title: Judgment, Discernment, and Obedience
objective: Understand how Jesus concludes the Sermon on the Mount with calls to self-examination, persistent prayer, discernment, and obedient action

required_passages.text: Judge not, that you be not judged. For with the judgment you pronounce you will be judged, and with the measure you use it will be measured to you. Why do you see the speck that is in your brother's eye, but do not notice the log that is in your own eye? Or how can you say to your brother, 'Let me take the speck out of your eye,' when there is the log in your own eye? You hypocrite, first take the log out of your own eye, and then you will see clearly to take the speck out of your brother's eye. Do not give dogs what is holy, and do not throw your pearls before pigs, lest they trample them underfoot and turn to attack you. Ask, and it will be given to you; seek, and you will find; knock, and it will be opened to you. For everyone who asks receives, and the one who seeks finds, and to the one who knocks it will be opened. Or which one of you, if his son asks him for bread, will give him a stone? Or if he asks for a fish, will give him a serpent? If you then, who are evil, know how to give good gifts to your children, how much more will your Father who is in heaven give good things to those who ask him! So whatever you wish that others would do to you, do also to them, for this is the Law and the Prophets. Enter by the narrow gate. For the gate is wide and the way is easy that leads to destruction, and those who enter by it are many. For the gate is narrow and the way is hard that leads to life, and those who find it are few. Beware of false prophets, who come to you in sheep's clothing but inwardly are ravenous wolves. You will recognize them by their fruits. Are grapes gathered from thornbushes, or figs from thistles? So, every healthy tree bears good fruit, but the diseased tree bears bad fruit. A healthy tree cannot bear bad fruit, nor can a diseased tree bear good fruit. Every tree that does not bear good fruit is cut down and thrown into the fire. Thus you will recognize them by their fruits. Not everyone who says to me, 'Lord, Lord,' will enter the kingdom of heaven, but the one who does the will of my Father who is in heaven. On that day many will say to me, 'Lord, Lord, did we not prophesy in your name, and cast out demons in your name, and do many mighty works in your name?' And then will I declare to them, 'I never knew you; depart from me, you workers of lawlessness.' Everyone then who hears these words of mine and does them will be like a wise man who built his house on the rock. And the rain fell, and the floods came, and the winds blew and beat on that house, but it did not fall, because it had been founded on the rock. And everyone who hears these words of mine and does not do them will be like a foolish man who built his house on the sand. And the rain fell, and the floods came, and the winds blew and beat against that house, and it fell, and great was the fall of it. And when Jesus finished these sayings, the crowds were astonished at his teaching, for he was teaching them as one who had authority, and not as their scribes.

segments.audio_script: Welcome to lesson nine. In lesson eight Jesus taught about secret devotion, the Lord's Prayer, and freedom from anxiety through trust in the Father. Today we reach the final section of the Sermon on the Mount. Jesus brings it to a close with five urgent movements: a warning against hypocritical judgment, an invitation to persistent prayer, the Golden Rule, a call to walk the narrow way, a warning about false prophets, and the parable of two builders. Each one presses toward a single question: will you actually do what you have heard?

Matthew chapter 7, verses 1 through 29. Judge not, that you be not judged. For with the judgment you pronounce you will be judged, and with the measure you use it will be measured to you. Why do you see the speck that is in your brother's eye, but do not notice the log that is in your own eye? You hypocrite, first take the log out of your own eye, and then you will see clearly to take the speck out of your brother's eye. Do not give dogs what is holy, and do not throw your pearls before pigs. Ask, and it will be given to you; seek, and you will find; knock, and it will be opened to you. For everyone who asks receives, and the one who seeks finds, and to the one who knocks it will be opened. If you then, who are evil, know how to give good gifts to your children, how much more will your Father who is in heaven give good things to those who ask him! So whatever you wish that others would do to you, do also to them, for this is the Law and the Prophets. Enter by the narrow gate. For the gate is wide and the way is easy that leads to destruction, and those who enter by it are many. For the gate is narrow and the way is hard that leads to life, and those who find it are few. Beware of false prophets, who come to you in sheep's clothing but inwardly are ravenous wolves. You will recognize them by their fruits. Not everyone who says to me, Lord, Lord, will enter the kingdom of heaven, but the one who does the will of my Father who is in heaven. And then will I declare to them, I never knew you; depart from me, you workers of lawlessness. Everyone then who hears these words of mine and does them will be like a wise man who built his house on the rock. And the rain fell, and the floods came, and the winds blew and beat on that house, but it did not fall, because it had been founded on the rock. And everyone who hears these words of mine and does not do them will be like a foolish man who built his house on the sand. And it fell, and great was the fall of it. And when Jesus finished these sayings, the crowds were astonished at his teaching, for he was teaching them as one who had authority, and not as their scribes.

The command judge not is one of the most frequently quoted and most frequently misunderstood verses in the Sermon. Jesus is not forbidding discernment or moral evaluation. The same chapter tells his listeners to recognise false prophets by their fruit, which requires judgement. What he forbids is the hypocritical posture of judging others while remaining blind to your own greater failings. The log and the speck image is deliberately absurd — a plank sticking from one's eye — to expose how out of proportion self-righteous criticism becomes when the critic refuses to examine themselves. The same chapter then demands active discernment: test prophets by their fruit, enter the narrow gate rather than the wide one, recognise that not everyone who uses the name of Jesus actually belongs to him.

The structure of chapter 7 moves from self-examination to prayer to ethics to discernment to obedience. On prayer: Jesus describes a father who does not give his child a stone when bread is asked for. The argument is from the lesser to the greater — if sinful human parents naturally give good gifts, how much more will the perfect Father give good things to those who ask him. The Golden Rule, do to others what you would have them do to you, is presented not as a new innovation but as the summary of the entire Law and the Prophets. On false prophets: the fruit test matters because appearances can be deceiving. The warning at the end is severe — some will claim to have prophesied and done miracles in Jesus' name and he will say I never knew you. Genuine relationship, not impressive activity, is what counts. The parable of the two builders then places the entire Sermon's weight on a single hinge: hearing without doing leads to collapse when the storm arrives.

Three themes stand out. The first is self-examination before judgment. Kingdom people look inward before they look at others. The second is the danger of nominal profession. Calling Jesus Lord without doing the Father's will produces false assurance. It is possible to be deeply religious while being unknown by Jesus. The third theme is the absolute necessity of obedience. The Sermon does not end with an invitation to admire good teaching. It ends with a question: are you building on rock or sand? The difference is not intellectual agreement with Jesus' words but actual, practical obedience to them.

Review question: According to Jesus' parable at the end of the Sermon on the Mount, what is the difference between the wise builder and the foolish builder? Answer: The wise builder hears Jesus' words and does them; the foolish builder hears the same words but does not do them.
segments.correct_answer: The wise builder hears and does; the foolish builder hears but does not do
acceptable_variants: one acts on what jesus says the other just listens
the wise man obeys jesus's words the foolish man does not
hearing and doing versus hearing without obeying
the difference is obedience: doing the words of jesus

segments.audio_script: You have completed lesson nine. The Sermon on the Mount is finished. The crowds were astonished because Jesus taught with authority — not citing other rabbis but speaking as the source himself. In lesson ten we move into Matthew's account of miracles, where Jesus will demonstrate the same authority through healing, calming storms, and driving out demons.

vocabulary.definition: Jesus' metaphor for the demanding path of genuine discipleship, contrasted with the wide gate that leads to destruction; the narrowness refers to the costliness of true commitment, not the size of the group

Teachers who appear trustworthy on the surface but lead people away from truth; Jesus says they are identified not by their claims or their miracles but by the fruit their lives and ministries produce over time
